action.hypothesis: A softer JumpReLU gate may recover stable throughput and improve proxy FVU versus the failed sharp-gating attempt by widening the bandwidth around the current best K=128 anchor without changing architecture or optimizer.
action.summary: Stay on the best known working JumpReLU K=128 configuration and run a single softer-gating proxy check by increasing gate bandwidth slightly, keeping the round focused on stability recovery and a clean read on threshold dynamics.
action.self_review: This stays within crash-recovery constraints and makes only a minor parameter adjustment on the current best working family. The main risk is that bandwidth softening may be too small to move FVU materially, but it is the safest informative step after consecutive crashes and the prior perf regression.
notes_to_memory: Round 8 used crash-recovery param-only mode and stayed on the best working JumpReLU K=128 anchor.
Tested a softer JumpReLU gate by widening bandwidth from 0.1 to 0.15 while keeping the threshold at 0.15 to separate stability effects from architecture changes.
This round is intended to check whether the round-3 regression came from overly sharp gating rather than from JumpReLU itself.
next_hypotheses: If wider-bandwidth JumpReLU improves proxy FVU with normal throughput, continue stabilizing within JumpReLU with another small gate-shape adjustment before any K reduction.
If throughput recovers but quality is flat or worse, try a small LR adjustment on the best JumpReLU anchor rather than more aggressive threshold changes.
If this softer-gating proxy still underperforms, pause JumpReLU-local sweeps until crash recovery ends and then revisit broader architecture exploration.
